sourceMovie.title: Mulan
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit Mulan for the same reason it broke through in 1998: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a family rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Tony Bancroft keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The animation and family blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: Moana
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Mulan clicked for you, Moana (2016) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and family, with comic timing and sharper banter. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

recommendations.title: Tangled
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If Mulan clicked for you, Tangled (2010) is a strong next stop: both trade in animation and family, with earned warmth without mush. Expect a different story, but a similar willingness to go big when the moment demands it.

faq.question: What should I watch after Mulan?
faq.answer: Try Moana, Tangled, Madagascar: Escape 2 Africa—each shares DNA with Mulan while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is Mulan?
faq.answer: Mulan (1998) is best described as Animation, Family, Adventure. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is Mulan part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "Mulan Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
